coredata.dc:title: Two-step förster resonance energy transfer amplification for ratiometric detection of pathogenic bacteria in food samples
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Zongbing
Xu, Huanwen
Feng, Qiumei
Zhang, Zifeng
Miao, Xiangmin
coredata.dc:description: We described a two-step förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) system for ratiometric Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) detection based on a dual-recognition proximity binding-induced toehold strand displacement reactions (TSDR). Ru(bpy)3 2+ and platinum nanoparticles (Pt NPs) labeled DNA (Ru-S3 and Pt NPs-S4) hybridized to enable the occurrence of the primary FRET using Ru(bpy)3 2+ as the energy donor and Pt NPs as the energy acceptor. TSDR happened by integrating vancomycin hydrochloride labeled S1 (Van-S1) and gold nanoclusters labeled S2-aptamer (Au NCs-S2-aptamer) with S. aureus. The single DNA segments of Van-S1 bond to the terminal toehold of Ru-S3, displacing Pt-S4, inducing the secondary FRET using Au NCs as the energy donor and Ru(bpy)3 2+ as the energy acceptor. This two-step FRET system efficiently improved the reaction efficiency of S. aureus with a detection limit of 1.0 CFU/mL. Furthermore, satisfactory results obtained while detecting S. aureus in food samples, indicating a great potential for food analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Staphylococcus aureus
Two-step förster resonance energy transfer
Ru(bpy)3 2+
Gold nanoclusters
Dual-recognition

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Au NCs and Pt NPs modified DNA
Assay of S. Aureus in real samples
Analysis of S. aureus in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21705062 and 21305053 ). The project funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions ( PAPD ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image and (B) DLS result of Au NCs; (C) UV–vis spectra of Au NCs (a) and Au NCs-S2-aptamer (b); (D) TEM image and (E) DLS result of Pt NPs; (F) UV–vis spectra of Pt NPs (a) and Pt NPs-S4 (b).

(A) PAGE analysis of various samples. Lane 1: DNA marker, lane 2: S1, lane 3: S2, lane 4: S1 and S2, lane 5: the mixture of Van-S1, S2-aptamer and S. aureus; (B) PAGE analysis of TSDR. Lane 1: DNA marker, lane 2: S3, lane 3: S4, lane 4: S3 and S4, lane 5: S3/S4 composite incubated with S1/S2/S. aureus.

(A) The fluorescence spectrum of Au NCs-S2-aptamer (blue curve) and the UV–vis spectrum of Ru-S3 (red curve); (B) Fluorescence spectral responses of Ru-S3 (a), the mixture of Ru-S3 and Pt NPs-S4 (b), Au NCs-S2-aptamer (c), and Ru-S3/Pt NPs-S4 composite incubated with Van-S1/Au NCs-S2-aptamer/S. aureus (d); (C) Fluorescence microscope images of the mixture of Van-S1, Au NCs-S2-aptamer and S. aureus, and Ru-S3/Pt NPs-S4 composite incubated with Van-S1/Au NCs-S2-aptamer/S. aureus (D); (E) and (F) were the bright fields of S. aureus accompanied with C and D. Scale bar was 20 μm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence spectra of Au NCs and Ru(bpy)3 2+ after the addition of different concentrations of S. aureus (from a to h: 5, 102, 103, 104,105,106, 107and 108 CFU/mL). (B) Fluorescence spectra of Au NCs (black) and Ru(bpy)3 2+ (red) with the logarithm of S. aureus concentrations; (C) Calibration curve for the linear relation between the logarithm of F450 nm/F610 nm and the logarithm of S. aureus concentrations; (D) Stability of the sensor after storing for 3 h with 105 CFU/mL S. aureus. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Bar graph of F450 nm/F610 nm (A) and the fluorescence microscopic images (B) of different samples: S. typhimurium (A), B. subtilis (B), E. coli (C), and S. aureus (D). Scale bar = 20 μm. The concentration of each pathogenic bacterium was 105 CFU/mL.
Schematic illustration for S. aureus detection using two-step FRET system.
Adding and recovery experiments for S. aureus in food samples.

head.ce:title: Two-step förster resonance energy transfer amplification for ratiometric detection of pathogenic bacteria in food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Science, Jiangsu Normal University, Xuzhou 221116, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life Science, Jiangsu Normal University, Xuzhou 221116, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Jiangsu Normal University, Xuzhou 221116, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Jiangsu Normal University, Xuzhou 221116, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We described a two-step förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) system for ratiometric Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) detection based on a dual-recognition proximity binding-induced toehold strand displacement reactions (TSDR). Ru(bpy)3 2+ and platinum nanoparticles (Pt NPs) labeled DNA (Ru-S3 and Pt NPs-S4) hybridized to enable the occurrence of the primary FRET using Ru(bpy)3 2+ as the energy donor and Pt NPs as the energy acceptor. TSDR happened by integrating vancomycin hydrochloride labeled S1 (Van-S1) and gold nanoclusters labeled S2-aptamer (Au NCs-S2-aptamer) with S. aureus. The single DNA segments of Van-S1 bond to the terminal toehold of Ru-S3, displacing Pt-S4, inducing the secondary FRET using Au NCs as the energy donor and Ru(bpy)3 2+ as the energy acceptor. This two-step FRET system efficiently improved the reaction efficiency of S. aureus with a detection limit of 1.0 CFU/mL. Furthermore, satisfactory results obtained while detecting S. aureus in food samples, indicating a great potential for food analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Pathogenic bacterial infections can cause fatal infectious diseases to poultry and human beings through food or contaminated water, and thus pose a serious threat to public health worldwide (Zhang & Xu, 2003). Among the major pathogen bacteria including Salmonella typhimurium (Sal. typhi), Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis), Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), S. aureus is one of the important foodborne or waterborne pathogen, which often leads to a wide range of foodborne diseases such as sexually transmitted infections, urinary tract infections, and healthcare-associated infections (Yu et al., 2017; Scallan et al., 2011; Gliddon, Herberg, Levin, & Kaforou, 2018; Organization, 2016). Thus, it is urgent to develop sensitive and accurate techniques for the early detection of S. aureus in human and food samples.

Over the past decades, numerous strategies have been developed for the detection of S. aureus. In particular, the conventional methods for S. aureus detection included polymerase chain reaction (PCR) (Effendi, Hisyam, Hastutiek, & Tyasningsih, 2019; Shylaja Ramlal, Kingston, & Batra, 2018), culture-based tests (Noor et al., 2018), and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) (Hu, Liu, & Zhang, 2019). Nevertheless, these strategies suffered from shortcomings such as long culture times, low sensitivity and complicated sample preparation. Alternatively, biosensors technology provided possibilities for the rapid and sensitive detection of S. aureus coupled with colorimetry (Zhang et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2021), fluorescence (Liu et al., 2022; Chen et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2018), electrochemistry (Cai et al., 2021a; Nemr et al., 2019; Cai et al., 2021b; Hu et al., 2021) and electrochemiluminescence (Liu et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2022). As one type of biosensing technologies, Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET)-based fluorescence method gained great attention on account of its sensitivity and simplicity.

FRET was a nonradiative energy-transfer process, in which the energy of excited fluorescent donor transfered to an acceptor within 10 nm of the distance (Li et al., 2021; Nguyen, Chern, Baer, Galagan, & Dennis, 2020; Medintz and Hildebrandt, 2013). Particularly, FRET-based sensing system mainly divided into two types according to the labeling of the signal probe. The first type was the conventional single-dye probe system, in which the emission intensities of energy donors were depended on the concentration of target. For instance, gold nanocluster-gold nanoparticles (Yu et al., 2017), carbon dot-gold nanoparticles (Xu et al., 2018), silver nanocluster-gold nanoparticles (Miao et al., 2018) and Cy5-gold nanoperticles (Hu et al., 2018), donor–acceptor pairs have widely applied in the construction of biosensor. Nevertheless, the single-dye probe-based system was susceptible to detection environment, and often suffered from false positive signals. To overcome these weaknesses, two-dye probe-based system have been developed by recording the fluorescence changes between the donor and acceptor in homogeneous assays using the ratiometric signal readout of FRET (Lee et al., 2015; Hu, Liu, & Zhang, 2019), avoiding the false positive signals and improving the specificity of the sensing system. Such inherent advantages enabled ratiometric FRET sensing platform to have a great application in biosensing (Zhang et al., 2019; Watabe, Terai, Sumiyama, & Matsuda, 2020; Ouyang et al., 2021; Afzalinia, & Mirzaee, 2020; Sebastián et al., 2018) and bioimaging (Lei et al., 2019; Bhunia et al., 2021).

Combining the advantages of FRET, a novel two-step FRET-based fluorescent sensor was constructed for the ratiometric detection of S. aureus based on a dual-recognition proximity binding-induced TSDR. As shown in Scheme 1 , the first FRET happened based on the partial hybridization between D* segment of Ru-S3 and D segment of Pt NPs-S4, quenching the initial signal of Ru(bpy)3 2+ by Pt NPs. Whereas, the simultaneous binding of S. aureus to Van-S1 and Au NCs-S2-aptamer could bring the complementary hybridization of B with B* section. Subsequently, T segment of Van-S1 bond to the terminal toehold region of Ru-S3 (T*), displacing Pt NPs-S4 through TSDR. The secondary high FRET efficiency between Au NCs donor and Ru(bpy)3 2+ acceptor was obtained. By measuring the fluorescence emission ratio of donor and acceptor (FD/FA), the proposed sensor achieved the quantitative detection of S. aureus with high sensitivity, selectivity and reliability.
Scheme 1.

Au NCs were synthetized according to the literature method (Xie, Zheng, & Ying, 2019). Brieflly, 5.0 mL of bovine serum albumin (BSA, 50 mg/mL) solution was added into 5 mL of 10 mM HAuCl4 solution, and stirred at 37 ℃ for 2 min. Subsequently, 0.5 mL of NaOH solution (1.0 M) was added into the mixture, stirring for 12 h at 37 ℃ until the color of the solution changed from yellow to brown. Followed by dialyzing and purifying in deionized water, the resulting Au NCs was stored at 4 ℃ before using.

Pt NPs were obtained by a simple one-step reaction method (Zhang, Xu, & Chen, 2013). 10 mL of citrate sodium (38.8 mM) and 50 mL of 1 mM H2PtCl6 were mixed, and followed by boiling them for 30 min until the solution varied from clear to black. Then, the obtained colloidal solution was cooled to room temperature. Finally, the prepared Pt NPs was dialyzed in deionized water, and stored at 4 ℃ before using.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Au NCs and Pt NPs modified DNA
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, S2-aptamer and S4 (5 OD) were diluted in 60 μL of acetate buffer solution (10 mM, pH 5.0), respectively, followed by adding 11 μL of 20 mM tris-(2-carboxethyl)-phosphine hydrochloride (TCEP) for 1.5 h to reduce the disulfide bonds separately. Au NCs-S2-aptamer was prepared by adding freshly deprotected S2-aptamer to 4.0 mL of Au NCs, and then incubated at 25 ℃ for 16 h. Then, centrifugation was constructed at 10000 rpm to remove the excess reagents for three times (each time for 15 min). Pt NPs-S4 was prepared with a same procedure of Au NCs-S2-aptamer.

Briefly, N-hydroxy succinimide (NHS) and N-(3-dimethyl aminopropyl)-N-ethylcarbodiimidehydrochloride (EDC) were added into 1 mL of Van solution (1 % w/v), incubating at 30 ℃ for 1 h. Then, 69 μL of S1 was added to the above mixture, which remained incubating at 30 ℃ for 24 h. After centrifugation at 8000 rpm and purification with Millipore (3 kDa) using PBS for three times, the prepared Van-S1 was stored at 4 ℃ before using.

S. aureus was collected from Luria-Bertani culture medium and diluted to different concentrations using SELEX buffer (pH 8.0, containing 5 mM KCl,1 mM CaCl2, 2 mM MgCl2, 150 mM NaC1). Excess Van-S1 and Au NCs-S2-aptamer (1:1 ratio) were added into 5, 102, 103, 104, 105, 106, 107and 108 CFU/mL of S. aureus, respectively, and followed by incubating at 37 °C for 30 min. Then, the reaction solutions were centrifugated at 3500 rpm for three times (each time for 5 min) to remove the unreacted Van-S1 and Au NCs-S2-aptamer. Ru-S3 and Pt NPs-S4 (1:1 ratio) were incubated at 37 °C for 1 h, and then they were added into the above S. aureus reaction solutions, respectively. After incubating for 30 min at 37 °C, the S. aureus was harvested by centrifugation (3,500 rpm/5 min) and rinsed with PBS (pH = 7.0) for three times. Ultimately, the fluorescence spectra of S. aureus were measured for quantitative analysis with a single-excitation wavelength at 350 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of S. Aureus in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To estimate the practical applications of the sensor, recovery testing was examined by spiking S. aureus into food samples. The fresh milk was obtained from a local supermarket, and then centrifugated at 11000 rpm/min to remove the fat layer formed on the surface, diluted for 5-fold before using. The water and white noodles soup samples were from the dining room of Jiangsu Normal University (Jiangsu, Xuzhou), the white noodles soup was centrifugated at 13000 rpm/min to obtain the supernate for the next S. aureus experiment. Then, the spiked samples with 10, 103 and 107 CFU/mL S. aureus were measured by the proposed method.

The characteristics of the prepared Au NCs were estimated using transmission electron microscopic (TEM), dynamic light scattering (DLS) and UV–vis spectra, respectively. As displayed in Fig. 1 A, the Au NCs were well dispersed, and the average size of them examined from DLS was about 3.5 nm (Fig. 1B). However, after the modification of Au NCs with S2-aptamer, the average diameter increased to 24.5 nm (Fig. S1). Besides, compared with the UV–vis spectrum of Au NCs (curve a), Au NCs-S2-aptamer exhibited a new characteristic adsorption band at 260 nm (curve b, Fig. 1C), indicating the formation of Au NCs-S2-aptamer. Fig. 1D showed the TEM image of Pt NPs with a spherical structure and uniform distribution. The average diameter of Pt NPs was about 3 nm (Fig. 1E). Further, the UV–vis adsorption spectra were also exploited to prove the formation of Pt NPs-S4 (Fig. 1F). Similar to curve b of Fig. 1C, a new characteristic absorption peak of S4 was observed at 260 nm (curve b). Concomitantly, the average diameter of Pt NPs-S4 increased to 9.8 nm (Fig. S2). Meanwhile, the UV–vis spectrum of Ru-S3 was also shown in Fig. S3. Two characteristic absorption peaks of DNA (S4) at 260 nm and Ru(bpy)3 2+ at 456 nm were observed.

Polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) was applied for confirming the feasibility of the desired DNA assembly in this system. As shown in Fig. 2 A, lane 1 was the DNA marker. Lanes of 2 and 3 displayed a distinct single band of S1 and S2, respectively. Lane 4 loaded a mixture of S1 and S2, which displayed two distinct bands that corresponded to the S1 and S2. Furthermore, lane 5 loaded the upper supernatant after the centrifugation of the reaction products including Van-S1, S2-aptamer and S. aureus. No obvious band appeared in PAGE image, suggesting the specific recognition of Van-S1 and S2-aptamer with S. aureus, which presented at precipitation after centrifugation. In addition, TSDR, which is vital to FRET, was also certified using PAGE. As shown in Fig. 2B, lane 1 was DNA marker. Lane 4 was loaded with the mixture of S3 and S4, which absolutely ran slower than that of S3 in lane 2 and S4 in lane 3, respectively, indicating an efficient hybridization of S3 with S4 to form double-stranded DNA (S3/S4). After S3/S4 composite incubated with S1/S2/S. aureus complex and centrifugation, the upper supernatant was loaded in lane 5 and only one band appeared, consistenting with the band of S3 in lane 2. Such results indicated the effective happen of TSDR to replace S3, while S1, S2 and S4 were specific bond with S. aureus.

To study the feasibility of FRET, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Ru-S3 and the fluorescence emission spectrum of Au NCs-S2-aptamer were recorded, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the fluorescence emission spectrum of Au NCs-S2-aptamer appeared at 450 nm (curve blue), while the UV–vis absorption of Ru-S3 was observed at 457 nm (curve red). A considerable spectral overlap made an efficient FRET between Ru(bpy)3 2+ and Au NCs. Meanwhile, the fluorescence responses of the sensor under different conditions were shown in Fig. 3B. Ru-S3 exhibited an obvious fluorescence emission at 610 nm (curve a). After the hybridization of Ru-S3 with Pt NPs-S4, the fluorescence emission of Ru(bpy)3 2+ extremely decreased due to the quench of Pt NPs to Ru(bpy)3 2+ with a quenching efficiency of 70.2% (curve b). On the other hand, Au NCs-S2-aptamer exhibited a distinguished fluorescence emission at 450 nm (curve c). However, after Pt NPs-S4/Ru-S3 composite incubating with Van-S1/Au NCs-S2-aptamer in the presence of 105 CFU/mL S. aureus, the fluorescence emission of Au NCs at 450 nm decreased accompanied with an increased fluorescence emission of Ru(bpy)3 2+ at 610 nm (curve d), suggesting an efficient FRET occurred between Au NCs and Ru(bpy)3 2+. Moreover, confocal microscopy was constructed to further investigate the FRET behavior between Au NCs and Ru(bpy)3 2+ in S. aureus. As shown in Fig. 3E, obvious blue signals were observed for the mixture of Van-S1, Au NCs-S2-aptamer and S. aureus. Then, after S3/S4 composite incubating with the mixture of Van-S1, Au NCs-S2-aptamer and S. aureus, strong red signals appeared accompanying with the decrease of the blue signals (Fig. 3F), which was consistent with the fluorescence emission results in Fig. 3B. Fig. 3C and D presented the bright field of S. aureus in Fig. 3E and F, respectively.

To achieve the optimal experimental performance, the domain length of B-B* was optimized in Fig. S4, an efficient FRET from AuNCs (450 nm) to Ru(bpy)3 2+ (610 nm) obtained while the domain length of B-B* increased to 6 base-pair, which indicating 6 base-pair of B-B* could realize the effective hybridization between Van-S1 and Au NCs-S2-aptamer, accordingly the single-stranded DNA segments of them could form stable double-stranded DNA structure with Ru-S3 for the FRET behavior between Au NCs and Ru(bpy)3 2+. Meanwhile, the hybridization time between Ru-S3 and Pt NPs-S4 was optimized. As shown in Fig. S5, the fluorescence emission of Ru-S3 gradually decreased along with the increase of the hybridization time between Ru-S3 and Pt NPs-S4, and then tended to be stable after 30 min. Thus, 30 min was used as the optimal hybridization time. Meantime, Ru-S3 and Pt NPs-S4 (1:1 ratio) were used for the first step of FRET, which is the key for constructing the sensor, and thus the concentration of them was optimized in Fig. S6, the F450 nm/F610 nm value decreased gradually along with the increase of Ru-S3 and Pt NPs-S4 concentration, and then reached a plateau after 5 μΜ. It could be assigned to that 5 μΜ of them could obtained an optimal FRET efficiency with Au NCs-S2-aptamer. In addition, the reaction time of TSDR were also investigated, the logarithm of F450 nm/F610 nm value decreased gradually as the reaction time between the Ru-S3/Pt NPs-S4 composite and the Van-S1/Au NCs-S2-aptamer/S. aureus complex increased, and a stable ratio could be achieved at 30 min (Fig. S7), which was chosen as the optimal reaction time.

To evaluate the detection ability of the sensor, S. aureus with different concentrations were tested under the optimal conditions. As indicated from Fig. 4 A, with the increasing of S. aureus concentrations, the fluorescence emission of Au NCs at 450 nm decreased concomitant with an increasing fluorescence emission of Ru(bpy)3 2+ at 610 nm. Meanwhile, either the decrease of the fluorescence emission of Au NCs (a) or the increase of the fluorescence emission of Ru(bpy)3 2+ (b), all showed the good linear relationships with the logarithm of S. aureus concentrations (Fig. 4B). Moreover, a good linear relation was obtained between the logarithmic value of F450 nm/F610 nm and the logarithm of S. aureus concentrations in the range from 5 to 108 CFU/mL. The following equation was Lg(F450 nm/F610) = -0.1138 lg c + 0.5664 (R2 = 0.996), with a detection limit of 1.0 CFU/mL (Fig. 4C), which was comparable or even superiour to recently reported fluorescence-based methods for S. aureus detection (Table S2). Fig. 4D displayed the satisfactory stability of the sensor, where the fluorescence response of F450 nm/F610 nm with 105 CFU/mL S. aureus remained stable for 3 h and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was 1.65 %.

To evaluate the specificity of the sensor for S. aureus detection, different interferences, such as Salmonella typhimurium (S. typhimurium), Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis) and Escherichia coli (E. coli) were investigated by evaluating the F450 nm/F610 nm ratio to those of different bacteria. As shown in Fig. S8, no fluorescence signal in F450 nm/F610 nm were obtained for S. typhimurium, B. subtilis and E. coli, while the fluorescence ratio of F450 nm/F610 nm was 1.06 for S. aureus. Such result owing to the high recognition ability of Van and aptamer to S. aureus, making the TSDR effectively happened to realize the secondary FRET. Furthermore, the specificity of the sensor also tested using confocal microscopy. As depicted in Fig. 5 , no obvious fluorescence images were observed for S. typhimurium (A), B. subtilis (B) and E. coli (C), but strong red signals appeared with S. aureus (D). Such results illustrated that the developed sensor possessed an especial selectivity for the detection of S. aureus due to the application of S. aureus aptamer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of S. aureus in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The recovery of S. aureus with known concentrations in food and water samples was applied to assess the accuracy of the proposed sensor. Initially, the fresh milk was obtained from a local supermarket, and the water and soup samples were obtained from the dining room of Jiangsu Normal University (Jiangsu, Xuzhou). Then the obtained samples were used for the assay of 10, 103 and 107 CFU/mL S. aureus. The recovery and relative standard deviations (RSD) of S. aureus were shown in Table 1 , where the recovery and RSD ranged from 96.00% to 106.00% and 0.7% to 6.5%, respectively. These results clearly illustrated that the proposed sensor could be served as a potential tool for S. aureus detection in real samples.

In summary, we developed a two-step FRET system for the ratiometric detection of pathogenic bacteria based on a dual-recognition proximity binding-induced TSDR. In this study, the first step of FRET between Ru-S3 and Pt NPs-S4 could efficiently eliminate the background signal of Ru(bpy)3 2+, while the second step of FRET between Au NCs-S2-aptamer and Ru-S3 was directly proportional with S. aureus concentration. Such two-step FRET system effectively improved the reaction efficiency of S. aureus with a detection limit of 1.0 CFU/mL. Furthermore, the sensing platform displayed satisfactory results while detecting S. aureus in food samples, providing a promising monitor platform for food safety analysis.

Zongbing Li: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Huanwen Xu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Qiumei Feng: Conceptualization, Project administration, Supervision, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Zifeng Zhang: Conceptualization, Project administration, Supervision. Xiangmin Miao: Conceptualization, Project administration, Supervision, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21705062 and 21305053). The project funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2022.134492.
